introduction: An inflammation of the lung parenchyma Various bacterial species, fungi and viruses may cause pneumonia The setting in which infection is acquired could be a predictor of the infecting pathogen Bacterial Pneumonia: is defined as bacterial infection of the lung parenchyma associated with recently developed radiological shadowing which may be segmental, lobar or multi lobar. Types • Community Acquired Pneumonia (CAP) • Hospital Acquired pneumonia (HAP) • Ventilator Associated pneumonia (VAP) • Health care associated Pneumonia (HCAP) • Pneumonia in the immunocompromised • Aspiration pneumonia Common bacteria causing CAP: • Streptococcus pneumonia(Most common cause) • Mycoplasma pneumonia • Legionella pneumophilia • Chlamydia pneumonia • Haemophilus influenza • Staphylococcus aureus • Chlamydia psittaci • Coxiella burnetti • Klebsiella pneumonia • Actinomyces israelli • Haemophilus influenzae • Pseudomonas aeruginosa (usually implicated in nosocomial pneumonia)

features: Sudden onset fever, chills and rigors
Chest signs are very helpful depending on the phase of the inflammatory response
In Children: inability to feed or drink, cyanosis, alteration in level of consciousness
Score 1 point for any of the above features present
3 or more- manage in Hospital as severe pneumonia

non_drug: Oxygen to maintain PaO at or above 8kPa
IV fluids especially in severe cases
drug: Macrolide (azithromycin 500 mg stat, then 250 mg daily, or clarithromycin 500 mg twice daily for up to 14 days)
Consider Pneumocystis jiroveci in HIV patients: Co-trimoxazole
adverse_reactions_and_cautions: Co-amoxiclav: Nausea, diarrhoea, skin rashes, contraindicated in penicillin hypersensitive individuals
Cefuroxime: Nausea, vomiting, abdominal discomfort, headaches, rarely antibiotic associated colitis
Macrolides: Similar to those mentioned above but usually milder. Hepatotoxicity and antibiotic associated colitis are quite rare.

complications: Deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism
